```{r}
    plot.margin        = margin(10, 15, 10, 10)
  )
```

FGD groups also identified potential value-added opportunities from CFM resources (@fig-et_value_added). The overlap with the previous two questions is substantial, but it is interesting that the communities specifically identify honey processing and drying mushrooms, as well as timber processing. This suggests that the communities are aware that there value addition opportunities that they are currently not realising.

```{r}
#| label: fig-et_value_added
#| code-fold: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| error:   false
#| fig-cap: "Potential value-added opportunities identified by FGD groups."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5

prefix_va <- "economic_transformation_potential_value_added_opportunities_"

va_labels <- c(
  "timber_processing_e_g_furniture_carpentry_woodcrafts"                                                    = "Timber processing",
  "charcoal_briquetting_eco_friendly_alternatives_to_traditional_charcoal"                                  = "Charcoal briquettes",
  "honey_processing_and_packaging_e_g_branded_honey_beeswax_products"                                      = "Honey processing",
  "mushroom_drying_and_packaging_for_extended_shelf_life_and_market_access"                                 = "Dried mushrooms",
  "wild_fruit_processing_e_g_juices_jams_dried_fruits"                                                     = "Wild fruits",
  "caterpillar_preservation_and_packaging_e_g_dried_or_canned_for_sale"                                    = "Caterpillars",
  "chikanda"                                                                                                 = "Chikanda",
  "munkoyo"                                                                                                  = "Munkoyo",
  "lusala_busala"                                                                                            = "Lusala/Busala",
  "mungongo"                                                                                                 = "Mungongo",
  "devils_claw"                                                                                              = "Devil's claw",
  "palm_leaves"                                                                                              = "Palm leaves",
  "grass"                                                                                                    = "Grass",
  "fish"                                                                                                     = "Fish",
  "minerals"                                                                                                 = "Minerals",
  "bamboo_products_e_g_furniture_handicrafts_construction_materials"                                        = "Bamboo products",
  "medicinal_plant_extraction_e_g_herbal_teas_essential_oils_medicinal_creams"                              = "Medicinal plants",
  "handicrafts_and_woven_products_e_g_baskets_mats_textiles"                                                = "Handicrafts",
  "resin_and_gum_processing_e_g_adhesives_fragrances"                                                       = "Resins",
  "ecotourism_development_e_g_eco_lodges_cultural_experiences_tour_guiding"                                 = "Eco-tourism",
  "agroforestry_based_businesses_e_g_tree_nurseries_sustainable_agriculture"                                = "Agroforestry"
)

full_va_vars <- paste0(prefix_va, names(va_labels))

va_freq <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type %in% c("cfmg_female", "cfmg_male", "cfmg_youth")) |>
  select(all_of(full_va_vars)) |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(. == 1, na.rm = TRUE))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "freq") |>
  mutate(word = va_labels[sub(prefix_va, "", variable, fixed = TRUE)]) |>
  filter(freq > 0) |>
  select(word, freq)

set.seed(42)
ggplot(va_freq, aes(label = word, size = freq, colour = freq)) +
  geom_text_wordcloud(eccentricity = 0.65, seed = 42) +
  scale_size(range = c(3, 22)) +
  scale_colour_gradient(low = "#6ABFB8", high = "#1A6B63") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(plot.margin = margin(0, 0, 0, 0))
```

We asked the CFMG Executive FGDs to report which primary markets CFM products are sold into (@fig-et_markets). Evidently, most products are sold only at the local market level, with some also being transported to national markets.

```{r}
#| label: fig-et_markets
#| code-fold: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| error:   false
#| fig-cap: "Primary markets for CFM products, as reported by executive FGDs."
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4

market_labels <- c(
  "economic_transformation_cfm_products_primary_market_local_markets"    = "Local",
  "economic_transformation_cfm_products_primary_market_national_markets" = "National",
  "economic_transformation_cfm_products_primary_market_regional_markets" = "Regional",
  "economic_transformation_cfm_products_primary_market_eu_markets"       = "EU"
)

cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "cfmg_executive") |>
  select(all_of(names(market_labels))) |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(. == 1, na.rm = TRUE))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "n") |>
  mutate(market = factor(market_labels[variable],
                         levels = c("Local", "National", "Regional", "EU"))) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = market)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2A9D8F", width = 0.55) +
  geom_text(aes(label = n), hjust = -0.3, size = 5, colour = "grey30") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15)),
    name   = "Number of CFMGs"
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(limits = rev) +
  labs(y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 18) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    axis.line.x.bottom = element_line(colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5),
    axis.text          = element_text(colour = "grey20"),
    plot.margin        = margin(10, 25, 10, 10)
  )
```

### Opportunity costs
#### Precluded activities

The community female, male, and youth FGD groups identified economic activities that are precluded under CFM regulations (@fig-et_precluded). While many of these are self evident, the fact that NTFP harvesting is listed quite prominently is surprising. Whether this reflects a misunderstanding on the part of the communities or indeed that they are being told they cannot collect NTFPs is not clear.

```{r}
#| label: fig-et_precluded
#| code-fold: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| error:   false
#| fig-cap: "Economic activities precluded by CFM, as reported by FGD groups."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5

prefix_p <- "economic_transformation_economic_activities_precluded_cfm_"

precluded_labels <- c(
  "logging"                              = "Logging",
  "timber_harvesting"                    = "Timber harvesting",
  "hunting"                              = "Hunting",
  "charcoal_production"                  = "Charcoal",
  "commercial_mining"                    = "Mining",
  "farming"                              = "Farming",
  "livestock_grazing"                    = "Livestock grazing",
  "land_conversion"                      = "Land conversion",
  "introduction_of_invasive_species"     = "Invasive species",
  "ntfp_harvesting_of_forest_products"   = "NTFP harvesting",
  "fishing"                              = "Fishing",
  "beekeping_tradtional"                 = "Beekeeping",
  "fire_based_livelihood_activities"     = "Fire livelihoods"
)

full_p_vars <- paste0(prefix_p, names(precluded_labels))

p_freq <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type %in% c("cfmg_female", "cfmg_male", "cfmg_youth")) |>
  select(all_of(full_p_vars)) |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(. == 1, na.rm = TRUE))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "freq") |>
  mutate(word = precluded_labels[sub(prefix_p, "", variable, fixed = TRUE)]) |>
  filter(freq > 0) |>
  select(word, freq)

set.seed(42)
ggplot(p_freq, aes(label = word, size = freq, colour = freq)) +
  geom_text_wordcloud(eccentricity = 0.65, seed = 42) +
  scale_size(range = c(3, 22)) +
  scale_colour_gradient(low = "#F4A582", high = "#8B0000") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(plot.margin = margin(0, 0, 0, 0))
```


#### Other opportunity costs
FGD groups were asked whether CFM entails opportunity costs for the community (@fig-et_opp_costs). Interestingly, many of the FGDs did not recognise any opportunity costs. However, about a third of the FGDs did. The proportion of FGDs recognising opportunity costs was highest among community male FGDs and lowest among community female FGDs. However, none of the pairwise comparisons were significant.

```{r}
#| label: fig-et_opp_costs
#| code-fold: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| error:   false
#| fig-cap: "Whether CFM entails opportunity costs for the community, by FGD group."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5

cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type %in% c("cfmg_executive", "cfmg_female",
                                "cfmg_male", "cfmg_youth")) |>
  transmute(
    group = case_match(
      interview_type,
      "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
      "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
      "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
      "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
    ),
    response = case_when(
      economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm == "yes"          ~ "Yes",
      economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm == "no"           ~ "No",
      str_starts(economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm, "No_") ~ "No",
      economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm %in% c(
        "Community forest situated very far from community",
        "The Major livelihood for this community is fishing. They don't depend so much on the forest. They can still get materials for construction in the open areas"
      ) ~ "No",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(response) |>
  mutate(
    group    = factor(group, levels = c("Youth", "Male", "Female", "Executive")),
    response = factor(response, levels = c("Yes", "No"))
  ) |>
  count(group, response) |>
  mutate(n_signed = if_else(response == "No", -n, n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(y = group, x = n_signed, fill = response)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, colour = "white", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label  = n,
        hjust  = if_else(response == "No", -0.4, -0.4),
        colour = response),
    size = 4, fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("No" = "white", "Yes" = "grey25"),
                      guide = "none") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("No" = "#E07B54", "Yes" = "#2A9D8F"),
                    guide  = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = ~abs(.x),
                     expand = expansion(add = 3),
                     name   = "Number of FGDs") +
  scale_y_discrete(expand = expansion(add = 0.5)) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  labs(y = NULL, fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 18) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    axis.line.x.bottom = element_line(colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5),
    axis.text          = element_text(colour = "grey20"),
    legend.position    = "bottom",
    plot.margin        = margin(10, 25, 10, 10)
  )
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
### Opportunity costs – FGDs – Model methods
To examine differences among FGD types in whether they reported that CFM entails opportunity costs for the community, we employed a generalised linear mixed model (GLMM) with a binomial response and random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We used FGD type as the factor. We examined the significance of pairwise differences among FGD types using Tukey HSD.
:::

```{r}
#| label: model_et_fgd_opp_costs
#| code-fold: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| error:   false

test.data.opp <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type %in% c("cfmg_executive", "cfmg_female",
                                "cfmg_male", "cfmg_youth")) |>
  transmute(
    province, cfmg_name,
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Executive", "Female", "Male", "Youth")
    ),
    empl = case_when(
      economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm == "yes"           ~ 1L,
      economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm == "no"            ~ 0L,
      str_starts(economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm, "No_") ~ 0L,
      economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm %in% c(
        "Community forest situated very far from community",
        "The Major livelihood for this community is fishing. They don't depend so much on the forest. They can still get materials for construction in the open areas"
      ) ~ 0L,
      TRUE ~ NA_integer_
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(empl) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name))

M9 <- glmer(empl ~ interview_type + (1 | province) + (1 | province_cfmg),
            data = test.data.opp, family = binomial)

emm.M9      <- emmeans(M9, specs = ~ interview_type, type = "response")
pairwise.M9 <- pairs(emm.M9, adjust = "Tukey")
```

```{r}
#| label: M9_et_fgd_opp_costs
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| error:   false
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Opportunity costs – FGD model results (click to expand)"

summary(M9)
```

```{r}
#| label: pairwise_et_fgd_opp_costs
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| error:   false
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Opportunity costs – FGD pairwise comparisons (click to expand)"

summary(pairwise.M9)
```

Individual interviewees were also asked whether CFM entails opportunity costs. @tbl-et_individual_opp_costs shows the percentage reporting this broken down by interviewee role. Interestingly, the community traditional leaders and community members recognise opportunity costs less of the time than other individual interviewee types, although these differences were not significant.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-et_individual_opp_costs
#| code-fold: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| error:   false
#| tbl-cap: "Percentage of individual interviewees reporting that CFM entails opportunity costs for the community, by interviewee role."

cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>
  select(
    interviewee_role,
    empl = economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm
  ) |>
  mutate(
    interviewee_role = case_match(
      as.character(interviewee_role),
      "cfmg_member"      ~ "Community member",
      "cfmg_exec_member" ~ "CFMG executive member",
      "trad_leader"      ~ "Traditional leader",
      "dfo"              ~ "District Forestry Officer",
      "NGO_rep"          ~ "NGO representative"
    ),
    interviewee_role = factor(interviewee_role, levels = c(
      "Community member", "CFMG executive member", "Traditional leader",
      "District Forestry Officer", "NGO representative"
    )),
    empl = case_when(
      empl == "yes"           ~ 1,
      empl == "no"            ~ 0,
      str_starts(empl, "No_") ~ 0,
      TRUE                    ~ NA_real_
    )
  ) |>
  group_by(interviewee_role) |>
  summarise(
    n             = n(),
    Opp_costs     = mean(empl, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups       = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(interviewee_role) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    interviewee_role = "Role",
    n                = "n",
    Opp_costs        = "Report opportunity costs"
  ) |>
  fmt_percent(
    columns  = Opp_costs,
    decimals = 0
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title    = "Opportunity costs of CFM, as reported by individual interviewees",
    subtitle = "Values represent the % of positive responses. n = the total number of interview responses. A positive response indicates that the respondent considers CFM to entail opportunity costs for the community."
  ) |>
  opt_align_table_header(align = "left") |>
  cols_align(align = "left",   columns = interviewee_role) |>
  cols_align(align = "center", columns = c(n, Opp_costs)) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(align = "center"),
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = interviewee_role)
  ) |>
  sub_missing()
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
### Opportunity costs – Individual interviewees – Model methods
To examine differences among individual interviewee roles in whether they reported that CFM entails opportunity costs for the community, we employed a generalised linear mixed model (GLMM) with a binomial response and random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We included interviewee role, interviewee gender and interviewee age as factors. To examine pairwise differences among interviewee roles, we employed Tukey HSD.
:::

```{r}
#| label: model_et_individual_opp_costs
#| code-fold: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| error:   false

test.data.opp.ind <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    empl = case_when(
      economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm == "yes"           ~ 1,
      economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm == "no"            ~ 0,
      str_starts(economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm, "No_") ~ 0,
      TRUE                                                                 ~ NA_real_
    ),
    interviewee_gender = demo_information_interviewee_gender,
    interviewee_age    = demo_information_interviewee_age
  ) |>
  drop_na(empl) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name))

M10 <- glmer(empl ~ interviewee_role + interviewee_gender + interviewee_age +
               (1 | province) + (1 | province_cfmg),
             data = test.data.opp.ind, family = binomial)

emm.M10      <- emmeans(M10, specs = ~ interviewee_role, type = "response")
pairwise.M10 <- pairs(emm.M10, adjust = "Tukey")
```

```{r}
#| label: M10_et_individual_opp_costs
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| error:   false
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Opportunity costs – Individual model results (click to expand)"

summary(M10)
```

```{r}
#| label: pairwise_et_individual_opp_costs
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| error:   false
#| output-fold: true
#| output-summary: "Opportunity costs – Individual pairwise comparisons (click to expand)"

summary(pairwise.M10)
```

The types of opportunity costs identified are shown in @fig-et_opp_cost_types. Mentions of reduced access, land restrictions and land use conflict are by some margin the most frequently identified opportunity costs. However, it is also interesting that time costs and diverting community resources are also quite prevalent. CFM requires that community members meet which entails a time cost and community resources are diverted to managing the forest that might otherwise be spent on other activities, such as agriculture.

```{r}
#| label: fig-et_opp_cost_types
#| code-fold: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| error:   false
#| fig-cap: "Types of opportunity costs experienced under CFM."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5

prefix_oc <- "economic_transformation_main_opportunity_costs_experienced_"

oc_labels <- c(
  "reduced_access_to_forest_resources_e_g_firewood_building_materials"    = "Reduced access",
  "time_spent_on_cfm_activities_instead_of_income_generating_work"        = "Time cost",
  "conflict_with_other_land_resource_uses"                                = "Land use conflict",
  "restrictions_on_land_use_for_farming_or_other_activities"              = "Land restrictions",
  "economic_costs_from_diverting_community_resources_to_cfm"              = "Diverting resources"
)

full_oc_vars <- paste0(prefix_oc, names(oc_labels))

binary_oc <- cfmg |>
  select(all_of(full_oc_vars)) |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(. == 1, na.rm = TRUE))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "freq") |>
  mutate(word = oc_labels[sub(prefix_oc, "", variable, fixed = TRUE)]) |>
  filter(freq > 0) |>
  select(word, freq)

comment_oc <- cfmg |>
  select(text = economic_transformation_main_opportunity_costs_experienced_comment) |>
  filter(!is.na(text), trimws(text) != "") |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") |>
  mutate(word = case_match(
    word,
    c("charcoal", "production", "prohibition", "stopped",
      "restriction", "restrictions") ~ "Charcoal",
    "chitemene"                        ~ "Chitemene",
    "grazing"                          ~ "Grazing",
    "timber"                           ~ "Timber",
    "logging"                          ~ "Logging",
    "fruits"                           ~ "Fruits",
    "medicine"                         ~ "Medicinal plants",
    .default                           = word
  )) |>
  filter(!word %in% c("reduced", "involved", "traditional",
                       "interference", "access", "source",
                       "villages", "moment")) |>
  count(word, name = "freq")

wc_oc <- bind_rows(binary_oc, comment_oc) |>
  group_by(word) |>
  summarise(freq = sum(freq), .groups = "drop")

set.seed(42)
ggplot(wc_oc, aes(label = word, size = freq, colour = freq)) +
  geom_text_wordcloud(eccentricity = 0.65, seed = 42) +
  scale_size(range = c(3, 18)) +
  scale_colour_gradient(low = "#F4A582", high = "#8B0000") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(plot.margin = margin(0, 0, 0, 0))
```

## Discussion

Through the FGDs and the individual interviews, we investigated a diverse set of indicators of economic transformation. We covered access to natural resources for household use and sale, employment, income earning opportunities, including carbon finance and other sources of income, and opportunity costs.

Most FGD groups and individual interviewees indicated that CFM had not increased access to natural resources for either household use or sale. However, about a third of respondents considered that access to resources had improved under CFM. Given that the communities were involved in harvesting forest products before the instigation of CFM, it is not surprising that most respondents do not recognise any change. However, around one third of respondent groups and individuals felt that access had improved under CFM. This presumably reflects the formalisation of community rights to access forest resources.

A larger proportion of FGD groups considered that CFM had had a positive effect on full-time employment. However, the differences among different FGD types were highly significant. More community female groups suggested there was no change in employment and more community youth groups felt full-time formal employment opportunities had increased. We also showed that the numbers employed included many more men than women and, given that by far the most important employment roles were HFOs and Community Scouts, it is likely they also included a disproportionate number of youth, which would explain these differences in perception. Moreover, while CFM does appear to have increased formal employment, the numbers are quite paltry. However, this may reflect the fact that employment in forest product harvesting and processing is rarely formal or full-time. In short, while CMF does appear to have resulted in some new employment opportunities, these have been largely limited to HFO and Community Scout roles and have not benefited many women.

A large proportion of CFs in Zambia were formed on the promise of carbon dollars. However, only approximately one third of the CFs in our sample appeared to have benefited from carbon income at the time of the survey. The fact that a number of the FDG groups, especially youth groups, were unaware of the carbon agreement is concerning. It suggests that the carbon trading companies may have failed to conduct a proper FPIC process involving the entire community and that the CFMG Executives may not be communicating properly with their communities. Nevertheless, many carbon traders are clearly supporting communities. Most of the roles concern forest protection and agricultural extension for sustainable intensification, for example, climate smart agriculture. Both of these may be considered as essential to a carbon business plan. NTFPs and enterprise development were less often mentioned as a part of the carbon trader activities. This is perhaps an oversight on their part, because enhancing forest product value chains would increase the value of forest conservation for local communities.

The above results are backed up by the response to questions concerning other income earning opportunities. It seems CFM has not led to increased income from market linkages, alternative livelihoods, conservation revenue, forest product processing or ecotourism except for in a very few cases. Nevertheless, a wide variety of forest products clearly contribute to household use and some additional income. Although honey and mushrooms were most often identified as the most important source of additional income, another 10 NTFPs were mention as the most important at least once. However, these additional sources of income were identified in less than half of the CFs and only 13 identified a second rank. Thus, forest products are rarely being exploited as a source of additional income and if they are it is usually only one. Only a small number of CFMGs are partnering with an enterprise, such as an off-taker, and among those that do it almost exclusively concerns honey. Nonetheless, the communities identified a wide diversity of forest products that could potentially be harvested and processed to provide an additional source of income. Prominent among these were timber and wild fruit, as well as mushrooms and honey. In some cases timber harvesting may conflict with a carbon business plans, but not if plantations were established. The potential of CFM to support forest product enterprises is clearly massively under appreciated. 

Unfortunately, the information on incomes from CFM were not very informative. Some clearly included income from carbon while most did not and it seems that the CFMG Executives have little understanding of the value of forest products being harvested from their CFs. To get a more accurate picture of these values a more in depth PEN type survey is recommended.

The local communities identified a number of opportunity costs to CFM. Most of these pertained to activities that are precluded, such as those involving forest clearance. However, many groups also identified other opportunity costs, such as time costs and diverted resources. Recognition of opportunity costs is important to understand the cost-benefits of CFM for communities.

Overall, a positive picture of economic transformation emerges from the responses of the FGDs and individual interviews. While changes in indicators, such as employment or additional income from forest products, many not appear substantial, they are in a positive direction. Most CFMGs will also receive carbon income, even if this has only benefited around one third of CFMGs so far. However, what is also evident is that CFMG business plans are over-reliant on just carbon or sometimes carbon and honey. Substantial opportunities for developing much more diversfied business plans exist. These could include a range of different NTFPs and potentially also timber. In addition to simply increasing income earning opportunities, diversified business plans would also provide a buffer against fluctuating commodity prices and because different products are harvested at different times of the year, they can help stabilise income throughout the year as well.
